---
title: "DATA 622 — Homework 3: Classification"
author: "Sabina Baraili"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    geometry: margin=1in
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  fig-width: 6
  fig-height: 4
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, StandardScaler
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, roc_auc_score, brier_score_loss, log_loss
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")
RANDOM_STATE = 622
```

## Data Source Justification
This assignment explicitly directs students to the course lab dataset for the Bank Marketing classification task. I use the course-provided dataset (`bank_marketing_dataset.csv`) because it is the canonical source for this homework and is accompanied by the official dictionary and lab prompt.

Why this source is appropriate:
- **Matches assignment scope** (classification for marketing targeting).
- **Supports all required analyses** (EDA, temporal drift, epoch evaluation, ROC/calibration, lift/hit-rate).
- **Ensures reproducibility** against instructor references.
- **Avoids scope drift** from alternative datasets with incompatible variables.

## 1) Data and setup

```{python}
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/georgehagstrom/DATA622Spring2026/main/website/assignments/labs/labData/bank_marketing_dataset.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(url)

df[['y']].head()
```

```{python}
# target normalization
if 'y' in df.columns:
    df['y'] = df['y'].map({'yes':1,'no':0}).fillna(df['y']).astype(int)

# temporal vars (used for epochs only; excluded as predictors)
for col in ['year','month_numeric','day']:
    if col in df.columns:
        df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors='coerce')

print(df.shape)
print(df['y'].value_counts(normalize=True))
```

## 2) Problem 1 — Kaggle flaw (short answer)

A common flaw in top notebooks is **using post-contact leakage variables** (especially call `duration`) when modeling pre-call targeting decisions. That produces inflated performance but is not actionable at call-selection time.

## 3) Problem 2 — EDA, baseline, epochs, drift

```{python}
# train/test split with stratification on y
train_df, test_df = train_test_split(
    df, test_size=0.25, random_state=RANDOM_STATE, stratify=df['y']
)

# build epochs from year-month when available
if {'year','month_numeric'}.issubset(test_df.columns):
    test_df = test_df.copy()
    test_df['time_index'] = test_df['year']*100 + test_df['month_numeric']
    test_df['epoch'] = pd.qcut(test_df['time_index'], q=4, duplicates='drop')
else:
    # fallback: quantile split by row order
    test_df = test_df.copy().reset_index(drop=True)
    test_df['epoch'] = pd.qcut(test_df.index, q=4, duplicates='drop')

# compact epoch labels for cleaner tables/plots in PDF
epoch_vals = sorted(test_df['epoch'].astype(str).unique())
epoch_map = {v: f"E{i+1}" for i, v in enumerate(epoch_vals)}
test_df['epoch_label'] = test_df['epoch'].astype(str).map(epoch_map)

test_df['epoch_label'].value_counts().sort_index()
```

```{python}
# Exclude leakage/temporal columns from predictors
exclude_cols = {'y', 'duration', 'month_numeric', 'day', 'year'}
features = [c for c in df.columns if c not in exclude_cols]

X_train = train_df[features]
y_train = train_df['y']
X_test = test_df[features]
y_test = test_df['y']

cat_cols = X_train.select_dtypes(include=['object', 'category', 'bool']).columns.tolist()
num_cols = X_train.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns.tolist()

preprocess = ColumnTransformer([
    ('num', Pipeline([
        ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
        ('scaler', StandardScaler())
    ]), num_cols),
    ('cat', Pipeline([
        ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='most_frequent')),
        ('ohe', OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore'))
    ]), cat_cols)
])

baseline = Pipeline([
    ('prep', preprocess),
    ('clf', LogisticRegression(max_iter=3000))
])

_ = baseline.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

```{python}
def lift_hit_table(y_true, p, cuts=(0.1, 0.5)):
    out = []
    base_rate = y_true.mean()
    rank = np.argsort(-p)
    ys = y_true.iloc[rank].reset_index(drop=True)
    n = len(ys)
    for c in cuts:
        k = max(1, int(np.floor(c*n)))
        hit = ys.iloc[:k].mean()
        lift = hit/base_rate if base_rate > 0 else np.nan
        out.append({'top_pct': c, 'hit_rate': hit, 'lift': lift})
    return pd.DataFrame(out)

def eval_epoch(name, y_true, p):
    auc = roc_auc_score(y_true, p)
    brier = brier_score_loss(y_true, p)
    ll = log_loss(y_true, p)
    err = ((p >= 0.5).astype(int) != y_true).mean()
    return {'epoch': str(name), 'n': len(y_true), 'base_success': y_true.mean(), 'error_rate': err, 'auc': auc, 'brier': brier, 'log_loss': ll}
```

```{python}
# Evaluate by epoch + overall
rows = []
lift_tables = {}

p_all = baseline.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1]
rows.append(eval_epoch('ALL', y_test, p_all))
lift_tables['ALL'] = lift_hit_table(y_test.reset_index(drop=True), pd.Series(p_all))

for ep in sorted(test_df['epoch_label'].unique()):
    m = test_df['epoch_label'] == ep
    Xt = X_test.loc[m]
    yt = y_test.loc[m]
    pt = baseline.predict_proba(Xt)[:,1]
    rows.append(eval_epoch(ep, yt, pt))
    lift_tables[ep] = lift_hit_table(yt.reset_index(drop=True), pd.Series(pt))

epoch_metrics = pd.DataFrame(rows)
epoch_metrics[['epoch','n','base_success','error_rate','auc','brier','log_loss']].round(4)
```

```{python}
# ROC curves by epoch
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
for ep in sorted(test_df['epoch_label'].unique()):
    m = test_df['epoch_label'] == ep
    pt = baseline.predict_proba(X_test.loc[m])[:,1]
    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_test.loc[m], pt)
    auc = roc_auc_score(y_test.loc[m], pt)
    plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f"{ep} (AUC={auc:.3f})")

fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_test, p_all)
plt.plot(fpr, tpr, 'k--', label=f"ALL (AUC={roc_auc_score(y_test,p_all):.3f})")
plt.plot([0,1],[0,1],':', color='gray')
plt.xlabel('FPR'); plt.ylabel('TPR'); plt.title('ROC by Epoch')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Calibration by epoch
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
for ep in sorted(test_df['epoch_label'].unique()):
    m = test_df['epoch_label'] == ep
    pt = baseline.predict_proba(X_test.loc[m])[:,1]
    frac_pos, mean_pred = calibration_curve(y_test.loc[m], pt, n_bins=10)
    plt.plot(mean_pred, frac_pos, marker='o', label=str(ep))

plt.plot([0,1],[0,1],':', color='gray')
plt.xlabel('Predicted probability'); plt.ylabel('Observed frequency')
plt.title('Calibration by Epoch')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Lift curves
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
for ep, t in lift_tables.items():
    xs = [r/100 for r in range(1,101)]
    # smooth estimate using sorted probabilities per epoch
    if ep == 'ALL':
        yt = y_test.reset_index(drop=True)
        pt = pd.Series(p_all)
    else:
        m = test_df['epoch_label'] == ep
        yt = y_test.loc[m].reset_index(drop=True)
        pt = pd.Series(baseline.predict_proba(X_test.loc[m])[:,1])
    rank = np.argsort(-pt.values)
    ys = yt.iloc[rank].reset_index(drop=True)
    base = ys.mean()
    lifts = []
    n = len(ys)
    for x in xs:
        k = max(1, int(np.floor(x*n)))
        lifts.append(ys.iloc[:k].mean()/base if base>0 else np.nan)
    plt.plot(xs, lifts, label=str(ep))

plt.xlabel('Top x% targeted')
plt.ylabel('Lift')
plt.title('Lift Curves by Epoch')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# concise table for top 10% and top 50%
out = []
for ep, tab in lift_tables.items():
    x = tab.copy()
    x['epoch'] = ep
    out.append(x)
pd.concat(out, ignore_index=True)
```

## 4) Problem 3 — Interpretation

```{python}
# Fit logistic directly for coefficient extraction
Xtr_prep = preprocess.fit_transform(X_train)
Xte_prep = preprocess.transform(X_test)

feature_names = preprocess.get_feature_names_out()
logit = LogisticRegression(max_iter=3000)
_ = logit.fit(Xtr_prep, y_train)

coefs = pd.DataFrame({
    'feature': feature_names,
    'coef': logit.coef_.ravel()
})
coefs['odds_ratio'] = np.exp(coefs['coef'])
coefs['feature'] = coefs['feature'].astype(str).str.slice(0, 42)
coefs['odds_ratio'] = coefs['odds_ratio'].round(3)

top_pos = coefs.sort_values('odds_ratio', ascending=False).head(10)[['feature','odds_ratio']]
top_neg = coefs.sort_values('odds_ratio', ascending=True).head(10)[['feature','odds_ratio']]

print("Top positive odds-ratio predictors")
display(top_pos)
print("Top negative odds-ratio predictors")
display(top_neg)
```

### Marginal effects for contact method (cellular vs telephone baseline)

```{python}
def avg_contact_effect(model, X, contact_col='contact', baseline='telephone'):
    # Average predicted probability difference if everyone had contact=level vs baseline
    if contact_col not in X.columns:
        return pd.DataFrame({'contact_level': ['not_available'], 'avg_marginal_effect_vs_telephone': [np.nan]})

    levels = sorted(X[contact_col].dropna().astype(str).unique())
    rows = []

    X_base = X.copy()
    X_base[contact_col] = baseline
    p_base = model.predict_proba(X_base)[:, 1]

    for lv in levels:
        X_lv = X.copy()
        X_lv[contact_col] = lv
        p_lv = model.predict_proba(X_lv)[:, 1]
        rows.append({
            'contact_level': lv,
            'avg_marginal_effect_vs_telephone': float(np.mean(p_lv - p_base))
        })

    return pd.DataFrame(rows)

marginal_tables = []
for ep in sorted(test_df['epoch_label'].unique()):
    m = test_df['epoch_label'] == ep
    eff = avg_contact_effect(baseline, X_test.loc[m], contact_col='contact', baseline='telephone')
    eff['epoch'] = ep
    marginal_tables.append(eff)

marginal_effects = pd.concat(marginal_tables, ignore_index=True)
marginal_effects.round(4)
```

## 5) Problem 4 — Compare with alternative model (Naive Bayes)

```{python}
# Naive Bayes on one-hot transformed features
nb = GaussianNB()
_ = nb.fit(Xtr_prep.toarray() if hasattr(Xtr_prep, 'toarray') else Xtr_prep, y_train)

p_nb = nb.predict_proba(Xte_prep.toarray() if hasattr(Xte_prep, 'toarray') else Xte_prep)[:,1]

# Per-epoch comparison by log score
model_epoch_rows = []
for ep in sorted(test_df['epoch_label'].unique()):
    m = test_df['epoch_label'] == ep
    y_ep = y_test.loc[m]

    p_lr_ep = baseline.predict_proba(X_test.loc[m])[:,1]
    p_nb_ep = nb.predict_proba((Xte_prep[m.values].toarray() if hasattr(Xte_prep, 'toarray') else Xte_prep[m.values]))[:,1]

    model_epoch_rows.append({'epoch': ep, 'model': 'Logistic', 'log_loss': log_loss(y_ep, p_lr_ep), 'auc': roc_auc_score(y_ep, p_lr_ep)})
    model_epoch_rows.append({'epoch': ep, 'model': 'NaiveBayes', 'log_loss': log_loss(y_ep, p_nb_ep), 'auc': roc_auc_score(y_ep, p_nb_ep)})

model_epoch_compare = pd.DataFrame(model_epoch_rows)
model_epoch_compare.round(4)
```

```{python}
# winner by epoch (lower log_loss is better)
winners = model_epoch_compare.sort_values(['epoch', 'log_loss']).groupby('epoch', as_index=False).first()
winners
```

```{python}
# Lift/hit rate by epoch for both models (top 10% and top 50%)
lift_rows = []
for ep in sorted(test_df['epoch_label'].unique()):
    m = test_df['epoch_label'] == ep
    y_ep = y_test.loc[m].reset_index(drop=True)

    p_lr_ep = baseline.predict_proba(X_test.loc[m])[:,1]
    p_nb_ep = nb.predict_proba((Xte_prep[m.values].toarray() if hasattr(Xte_prep, 'toarray') else Xte_prep[m.values]))[:,1]

    t_lr = lift_hit_table(y_ep, pd.Series(p_lr_ep)); t_lr['model'] = 'Logistic'; t_lr['epoch'] = ep
    t_nb = lift_hit_table(y_ep, pd.Series(p_nb_ep)); t_nb['model'] = 'NaiveBayes'; t_nb['epoch'] = ep

    lift_rows.extend([t_lr, t_nb])

lift_epoch_compare = pd.concat(lift_rows, ignore_index=True)
lift_epoch_compare[['epoch','model','top_pct','hit_rate','lift']].round(4)
```

## 6) Conclusions (plain-language summary)

```{python}
best_overall = compare = pd.DataFrame([
    {'model':'Logistic', 'auc':roc_auc_score(y_test,p_all), 'brier':brier_score_loss(y_test,p_all), 'log_loss':log_loss(y_test,p_all)},
    {'model':'NaiveBayes', 'auc':roc_auc_score(y_test,p_nb), 'brier':brier_score_loss(y_test,p_nb), 'log_loss':log_loss(y_test,p_nb)}
]).sort_values('log_loss').iloc[0]

print("Best overall model by log-loss:", best_overall['model'])
print("AUC:", round(best_overall['auc'], 4), "| Log-loss:", round(best_overall['log_loss'], 4))

print("\nEpoch winners by log-loss:")
print(winners.to_string(index=False))

summary_lift = lift_epoch_compare.sort_values(['epoch','model','top_pct']).copy()
summary_lift[['top_pct','hit_rate','lift']] = summary_lift[['top_pct','hit_rate','lift']].round(3)
print("\nLift/Hit summary (top 10% and 50%):")
print(summary_lift.to_string(index=False))
```

In short: temporal drift is real in this dataset, so a single pooled score can hide performance swings. Evaluating by epoch gives a more realistic view of campaign risk. For operations, prioritize the model with consistently better epoch-level log-loss and use lift/hit-rate at top 10% and 50% targeting to set campaign size.
